title: MVVM — Model / View / ViewModel
objectives: Understand MVVM via a real-life analogy
Implement MVVM cleanly with ChangeNotifier / Riverpod / Bloc
Recognize modern Flutter apps as MVVM-flavored

sections.markdown:
**Think of MVVM as a modern airport check-in counter with a digital display board.**

- **The Model** is the backstage — flight schedules, gate assignments, weather delays — the ground truth. It doesn't format anything or handle passengers.
- **The ViewModel** is the airline's dispatcher: watches the backstage data, translates it into 'FLIGHT AA123 → NOW BOARDING GATE 4', announces changes, and hands ready-to-display strings to the display board.
- **The View** (the display board) shows whatever the dispatcher publishes. When the dispatcher updates 'boarding' → 'departed', the board changes automatically. It doesn't ASK for updates; it just reflects the latest data.

Crucially, the passengers (users) tap 'Check In' → their tap goes to the DISPATCHER (ViewModel), not directly to the backstage (Model). The dispatcher decides what to do, updates the Model, and the display refreshes.

**Why MVVM is the modern default:** the View doesn't have to know when to redraw — it BINDS to ViewModel state and auto-refreshes. That single change fixes 80% of MVC's mobile problems. It's why Android Jetpack shipped a `ViewModel` class, why WPF/UWP was built on it, why every Riverpod `Notifier` is essentially a ViewModel in disguise. **Modern Flutter apps written 'idiomatically' are basically MVVM whether they say so or not.**

**MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)** is an architectural pattern where the **ViewModel** sits between View and Model. It exposes **observable / reactive state** (via `ChangeNotifier`, `Notifier`, `Cubit`, `StreamController`, etc.). The **View** binds to that state declaratively and rebuilds when it changes. The **Model** is the domain / data layer, framework-free. **Data flow is unidirectional**: state flows View ← ViewModel ← Model; user intents flow View → ViewModel → Model.
## The three roles — with concrete Flutter examples

rows: Domain data, business rules, DTOs, persistence
UI state + input handlers + async orchestration
Rendering only; forwards taps to ViewModel

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — MVVM with plain `ChangeNotifier`
## MVVM in the four state-management libraries

**The insight to quote:** every popular Flutter state-management library is a flavor of MVVM. The 'M-V-VM' triangle stays the same; only the observability mechanism differs. Once you see this, choosing a library becomes a preference decision (codegen? boilerplate? ergonomics?), not an architectural one.
## MVVM vs MVC vs MVP — the tiny differences

rows: Controller pushes updates to View; View may observe Model
Presenter holds a reference to View; explicit method calls
ViewModel exposes observable state; View auto-binds
State is a single immutable value; reducer applies Intent

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: View mutates Model directly.** `context.read<CartModel>()._items.add(item)` — bypasses the ViewModel; hides business rules; unbreakable in test. Always go through a ViewModel method.

**Mistake 2: ViewModel imports `package:flutter/material.dart`.** The ViewModel should be UI-framework-agnostic (or at least widget-free). If it uses `BuildContext` or `Navigator`, testing requires widget infrastructure.
**Mistake 3: One giant ViewModel for the whole app.** Feature-scoped ViewModels beat mega-VMs. Split by screen, by responsibility, by user flow.
**Mistake 4: View reads Model fields.** If your widget accesses `vm.model.count`, you've leaked the Model into the View. Expose a `vm.count` getter on the ViewModel; keep the Model private.
**Mistake 5: Business logic in the View.** `if (loading && !error && user != null && user.age >= 18)` — that's a decision. Move it to a ViewModel getter (`vm.canProceed`).
## Best practices
**1. Keep ViewModels widget-free.** No `BuildContext`, no `Navigator.of`. Use callbacks or events for navigation.
**2. Expose UI-shaped state.** Don't leak DB rows or DTOs; expose `bool loading`, `String? error`, `List<UserSummary> users` — what the UI actually needs.
**3. Repositories under the ViewModel.** The ViewModel calls Repository, the Repository handles network + cache.
**4. Test the ViewModel like a plain Dart class.** No widget pumping. If you can't, your ViewModel has widget dependencies — refactor.
**5. Use sealed classes for state.** `sealed class UserState { Loading, Loaded(user), Error(msg) }` — compiler enforces exhaustive matching.
**6. Same architecture, different libraries.** MVVM works with any state-management library — Provider, Riverpod, Bloc, MobX are just observability mechanisms.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Counter MVVM.** Convert your MVC counter to MVVM with `ChangeNotifier`.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — User fetch.** Build the sample; verify no Flutter imports in ViewModel or Repository.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Test the ViewModel.** Pure Dart tests: `test('loads user', () async { … })` — no widget tree needed.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Same ViewModel, four libraries.** Same `UserViewModel` logic, wrapped in Provider / Riverpod / Bloc / MobX. Notice the ViewModel logic stays IDENTICAL.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Handle navigation from the ViewModel.** ViewModel emits an event; a top-level listener translates events to `Navigator.push`. No context in the ViewModel.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is MVVM?**

**A:** MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) is an architectural pattern where a **ViewModel** exposes UI-shaped observable state, the **View** binds declaratively to that state (auto-rebuild on change), and the **Model** holds domain data + business rules + persistence. Data flows unidirectionally: state flows View ← ViewModel ← Model; user intents flow the other way. Originated in WPF/UWP (Microsoft); popularized on Android by Jetpack's `ViewModel` class; ubiquitous in modern Flutter via `ChangeNotifier`, Riverpod, Bloc, MobX. **Modern Flutter apps written 'idiomatically' are almost always MVVM whether they say so or not.**

**Q2: How does MVVM differ from MVC?**

**A:** Both separate Model / View / mediator. **MVC** — Controller handles user input, updates Model, then TELLS the View to refresh. Often bidirectional coupling. **MVVM** — ViewModel exposes observable state; View BINDS to it declaratively. When state changes, the View rebuilds automatically without the ViewModel needing to know about the View. **Key differences:** (1) MVVM is **reactive** — View auto-refreshes on state change. (2) MVVM has **unidirectional data flow** — state flows one way; commands flow the other. (3) MVVM's ViewModel is **framework-independent** — testable as plain Dart. (4) MVVM's View **doesn't know about the Model** — only about the ViewModel's UI-shaped state.

**Q3: What does a ViewModel expose?**

**A:** UI-SHAPED state, not raw domain data. Examples: `bool loading`, `String? error`, `List<UserSummary> users`, `bool canSave`, `String buttonLabel`. Never `User` (that's the Model); never `Response<UserDto>` (that's the network layer). The ViewModel translates domain objects into whatever the View actually renders. It also exposes **input handlers** — methods the View calls when the user taps: `void onRefreshTapped()`, `Future<void> loadMore()`. **Rule:** if you replaced the View with a totally different UI (CLI, web, watch), the ViewModel should still work — because it's UI-agnostic.

**Q4: Where does the Repository fit in MVVM?**

**A:** Between ViewModel and the outside world. The **Repository** is the single source of truth for a data type — it combines API, cache, DB into one clean interface. The ViewModel calls the Repository (`await _repo.fetchUser(id)`), never Retrofit or Dio directly. This gives you: **(1) Testability** — mock the Repository, test the ViewModel with fake data. **(2) Cache logic in ONE place**, not scattered across screens. **(3) Reusability** — multiple ViewModels can share one Repository. **The layered pattern is:** View → ViewModel → Repository → (API / DB / Cache). This mirrors Google's official Android + Flutter architecture guidance.

**Q5: How does MVVM work with Flutter's declarative UI?**

**A:** Perfectly. Flutter's `build()` is a pure function of state; MVVM's ViewModel exposes the state. When state changes, Flutter's framework diffs the widget tree and updates minimally. **Concrete flow:** ViewModel `notifyListeners()` (or updates a `StateFlow` / emits a `state`) → the state-management library marks the observing widget dirty → Flutter re-runs `build()` → the tree re-computes → the framework applies minimal changes to the render tree. **Compare to imperative UI (UIKit, Android XML views):** you had to manually call `view.setText(...)`. In Flutter, the `build()` handles it. **This is why MVVM feels 'native' in Flutter** — the pattern and the framework co-evolved.

**Q6: What's the difference between a ViewModel and a Controller?**

**A:** Overlapping but not identical. **Controller (MVC)** — mediates user input, updates Model, may call View to refresh. Often bidirectional; sometimes tied to UI framework (iOS `UIViewController`). **ViewModel (MVVM)** — exposes observable state, handles user intents. UI-framework-agnostic. Doesn't push updates to View; View subscribes to state. In practice, if you take a mobile MVC Controller, remove all View references, and expose its state as reactive properties → you've built a ViewModel. That evolution is exactly what Android Jetpack did with its `ViewModel` class and what Flutter's state-management libraries encourage.

**Q7: Is BLoC an MVVM pattern?**

**A:** Yes, with a specific flavor. In BLoC: **Bloc/Cubit is the ViewModel** — it holds state and exposes it via a Stream. **State classes are the Model** (in the immutable-value-object sense) — plain Dart objects representing UI-shaped state. **BlocBuilder is the binding** — the View binds to state changes. **The 'event' concept** is BLoC's extra layer on top of vanilla MVVM: instead of the View calling arbitrary methods on the ViewModel (like `.increment()`), it dispatches named events. That's MVVM + explicit event log. **So:** BLoC is MVVM with unidirectional flow enforced by events. Same for Riverpod's `Notifier`, MobX's Store, Redux — all MVVM flavors.

## Real-world usage

- **99% of production Flutter apps** — implicitly MVVM whether they call it that or not.
- **Android Jetpack `ViewModel`** — MVVM built into the framework.
- **WPF / UWP / Xamarin** — MVVM from birth.
- **Vue.js, Knockout.js** — MVVM in the web world.
- **Google's official Flutter architecture guide** — MVVM + Repository.

## Advanced notes

- **`ChangeNotifier` vs `ValueNotifier`** — `ValueNotifier<T>` is a mini ViewModel holding one value.
- **State restoration** — `RestorableChangeNotifier` variants preserve state across process death.
- **Nested ViewModels** — one VM per screen, plus a top-level session/auth VM.
- **Testing** — plain Dart unit tests on the ViewModel; mock the Repository.
- **Navigation from ViewModel** — controversial; usually via a callback or event stream, not direct `Navigator.push`.
- **AsyncNotifier / AsyncValue** — Riverpod's async-first ViewModel style.
- **Cubit** — a minimalist ViewModel from the Bloc package.
- **Freezed for state** — sealed unions with generated equality + `copyWith`.
- **Reactive collections** — `ObservableList` in MobX, `StateNotifier<List<T>>` in Riverpod.
- **MVI as MVVM sibling** — Model-View-Intent is MVVM with an explicit `Intent` sealed hierarchy; used in Kotlin/Android.

flashcards.back: Model (data + rules), ViewModel (observable UI state + input handlers), View (binds declaratively).
One-way: state flows Model → ViewModel → View. Intents flow the other way.
View binds to observable state — no manual 'tell view to refresh'.
Between ViewModel and outside world. VM calls Repo; Repo owns API + cache.
UI-shaped state (bool loading, String? error, List of items) — not raw domain objects.
Idiomatic Flutter with any state library is MVVM in practice. The framework naturally fits it.

options: View auto-binds to observable state (no manual refresh)
Between ViewModel and outside world (API, DB, cache)
quiz.explain: Single source of truth for a data type.
